problem_summary: 2020 MCM Problem A (continuous): under global warming, model where two commercially important fish species (Atlantic herring and mackerel) migrate over the next ~50 years as North-East Atlantic / North Sea sea-surface temperatures (SST) rise, determine how long small Scottish fishing companies (vessels WITHOUT refrigeration) can keep harvesting, and recommend business strategies (relocate the home port vs. buy refrigerated vessels), including the consequences of fish crossing into neighboring (Norwegian) territorial waters. This team (#2007799) frames the deliverable as a consultant report plus a magazine article for Hook Line and Sinker.
models_used: SST prediction model: superposition of a power-law trend f(x)=a·x^b+c and an annual periodic term g(x)=p·sin(qx+r), giving pre(x)=p·sin(qx+r)+a·x^b+c. Fit per-block via nonlinear least squares on NOAA data from 2012 onward (deliberately discarding pre-2012 data as a 'gentle' regime). Best/worst climate cases generated by perturbing a,b,c.

Moore-neighborhood Cellular Automaton (8-neighbor) for fish-school migration on a 300x300-pixel grid partitioned into the same 7x7 temperature blocks. Two cell action modes per step: random walk when local temperature is tolerable, directed move toward optimal-temperature neighbor when threshold exceeded. Implemented in MATLAB.

Sigmoid (logistic) temperature-stimulus function p_stimulate = 1/(1+e^{-(|t_current - t_optimal| - 5)}) compared against a species threshold p_threshold to decide random-walk vs. directed-migration behavior.
Crowding/resource-competition rule: move probability scales with neighbor count (movchance = count/8 + 0.2) to emulate plankton resource allocation among schools.

Maximum fishing-range model: empirical vessel-speed estimation formula v = 1.84·(P/Δ)^0.237·√L (P engine power, Δ displacement, L length between perpendiculars), combined with fish freshness time to give D_max = t_fresh · v_vessel (108.6 km circle, ~74,066 km^2).

Fourth-order polynomial fits (position = p1·i^4 + p2·i^3 + p3·i^2 + p4·i) to the CA-derived school centroid trajectories (separate lon/lat fits per species) to turn discrete CA output into closed-form time-indexed coordinates.

Cost-benefit / revenue model comparing Option 1 (relocate port, no refrigeration) vs Option 2 (buy refrigerated vessel): round-trip period T(i)=2d(i)/v, annual revenue R(i)=(T_y/T(i))·W_max·S, with R1 = Σ R(i) − 10·C_rel (2020–2040) and R2 = Σ R(i) − a·C_boat (2020–2070), parameterized with real Scottish Fisheries Statistics prices (herring £444.75, mackerel £883.75) and costs (relocation £750k, refrigerated boat £220k).

Spatial intersection rule coupling the CA fish positions with each port's 108.6 km range disk to compute per-port 'elapsed time' until harvesting becomes impossible (Table 3), evaluated under best and worst SST cases.
key_techniques: Discretizing a continuous geographic domain into a 7x7 block grid and reusing that SAME grid across all sub-models (SST fit, CA cell space, port ranges) so outputs of one model feed directly into the next.
Per-block independent curve fitting: each of the 49 blocks gets its own pre(x) parameter set, turning a hard spatiotemporal field-prediction problem into 49 tractable 1-D regressions.
Deliberate data-window choice (post-2012 only) justified explicitly by a regime-change argument, trading historical coverage for trend cleanliness.
Coupling a stochastic agent-based model (CA) with deterministic physics inputs (SST field) — using randomness to capture migration 'chaos' while the temperature field supplies the directional drift.
Bridging discrete simulation output to analytic tractability by polynomial-fitting the CA centroid path, enabling closed-form distance/period/revenue computation in the economic model.
Scenario analysis via parameter perturbation (best/worst climate by adjusting a,b,c) instead of a separate climate model.
Real-data grounding throughout: NOAA SST, FishBase optimal/tolerance temperatures, Scottish Sea Fisheries Statistics for vessel specs, fish prices, and port locations.
One-factor-at-a-time sensitivity analysis on the three economic levers (vessel speed, payload W_max, fish price S), each swept linearly with the other two fixed.
Identifying a decision threshold / tipping point (year ~2040) where Option 1 becomes infeasible and the recommendation must switch to Option 2.
Surfacing a counter-intuitive but defensible finding (Shetland is initially poor but becomes the best long-term site as fish migrate north) — the kind of insight judges reward.
why_it_won: "It fully closes the loop the problem demanded: temperature -> species location -> harvest feasibility per port -> dollar-valued business strategy -> geopolitical (quota) consequence -> plain-language magazine article. Every required sub-question is answered with a concrete number (herring near Alesund/801 km NE, mackerel 268 km NW of Bergen, D_max=108.6 km, elapsed times 13.7 yr worst / 21.2 yr best average, 2040 strategy-switch point). The architecture is coherent and reusable: one 7x7 grid threads all models so results chain cleanly rather than being disconnected analyses. The CA + SST coupling is a genuinely creative, problem-appropriate choice for biological migration (group behavior with chaos/randomness) that judges find more compelling than a black-box regression, and the team explicitly flags it as their innovation. It is rigorously data-grounded (NOAA, FishBase, official Scottish fisheries statistics with real prices and vessel specs), so numbers feel credible rather than invented. It delivers actionable, ranked recommendations with a clear decision rule and a memorable non-obvious insight (Shetland's future upside), and it honestly enumerates its own limitations — the maturity judges associate with O-award teams. The magazine article is on-message, audience-appropriate, and concrete."
section_highlights: "Summary: dense and number-rich — states every headline result up front (migration endpoints with km distances, D_max, average elapsed times, the 2020-2040 vs 2040-2070 strategy split), exactly what a skimming judge wants. Assumptions: explicit, numbered, and each one simplifies a specific downstream model (no refrigeration -> freshness-limited range; constant fish population -> CA conserves cells; 24h operation -> clean period math). Notation table: complete symbol glossary before any modeling. Model architecture flows logically section-to-section, with the CA action-mode flowchart (Figure 6) making the agent logic legible. Visuals carry the argument: per-decade SST heatmaps (Fig 5), per-decade CA snapshots showing both species marching north (Fig 7), fitted trajectory curves (Fig 10), and side-by-side option-revenue charts (Fig 11). Model Evaluation section is candidly self-critical and lists four concrete flaws. Appendices include real MATLAB source for both the CA and the cost-benefit model plus all polynomial parameters and port coordinates — strong reproducibility signal. Magazine article restates findings in fisherman-friendly language with 5 numbered recommendations."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Unit error left uncorrected: vessel speed stated as 'v_vessel = 18.1 km/s' (and elsewhere km/h) — a glaring typo; 18.1 km/s is absurd, undermining polish even though the math used km/h.

SST model fits a power function a·x^b in DAYS over a 50-year extrapolation; the team itself notes time-series models 'distort' over that span, but a·x^b is monotonic and unbounded, so the long-range trend is essentially an unvalidated guess with no uncertainty band.

Discarding all pre-2012 data leaves very few annual points to fit 6 parameters per block, risking overfit; the 'best case' vs 'worst case' SST curves in Figure 9 look implausibly flat/constant, suggesting fitting or plotting problems.
CA is single-factor (temperature only) — explicitly ignores depth, currents, coastline, predators, spawning behavior, and food; migration realism is limited and the team concedes this.
Fish population assumed constant for 50 years (no recruitment/mortality/fishing pressure feedback), which decouples the economics from any stock-depletion reality.

Option 1 uses random relocation distances (unifrnd 0–108.6 km in the MATLAB code) rather than an optimal port-siting algorithm, injecting noise and arguably inflating Option 1 revenue — the team admits no optimal addressing was done.
Single representative vessel (L=24m, P=440kW, Δ=70t, t_fresh=6h) — fleet heterogeneity ignored, so D_max and all downstream economics hinge on one parameter set.
No discounting/NPV in the multi-decade cost-benefit model; revenues across 2020-2070 are summed in nominal terms, and the 'a=1 boat' assumption is simplistic.
Territorial-waters / Norwegian quota impact is only qualitatively discussed ('our model cannot specifically estimate the impact'), leaving one sub-question largely unmodeled.
Contents/section numbering is sloppy (two 'Section 5' headings, an 'xx model' placeholder left in the evaluation text), minor but visible editing lapses.
reusable_patterns: Shared discretization grid as a 'data bus': pick one spatial grid and force every sub-model (physical field, agent simulation, economic geography) onto it so outputs chain without re-gridding.

Trend+periodic superposition (monotonic trend term + sinusoid) as a quick, interpretable forecaster for seasonal climate/temperature signals — and generate best/worst scenarios by perturbing the trend coefficients rather than building a second model.

Cellular automaton for population/agent migration driven by an external scalar field, with a sigmoid stimulus-vs-threshold rule to switch between random-walk and directed-move behavior — a clean template for any 'organisms chase a comfortable environment' problem.
Polynomial-fit the centroid trajectory of a stochastic simulation to recover a closed-form path you can plug into downstream analytic models (distance, time, cost).
Freshness/decay-limited operating radius: D_max = t_perishable · v as a general 'reachable range' constraint for logistics/perishable-goods problems.

Two-option cost-benefit comparison with a per-period revenue formula R = (operating_time/round_trip_period)·capacity·price minus a fixed switching cost, then find the crossover year that flips the recommendation.
Scenario bracketing (best/worst) plus OFAT sensitivity sweeps on the few economic levers, each with the 'this is intuitive because...' narration that builds judge confidence.
Mine the model for a counter-intuitive, decision-relevant insight (here: the currently-weak Shetland port becomes the long-term winner) and feature it prominently.
Ground every parameter in a citable official/biological source (NOAA, FishBase, government fisheries statistics) so numbers read as evidence, not invention.

Ship runnable code + all fitted parameters + raw coordinates in the appendix for reproducibility, and write the lay-audience deliverable (magazine article) as a faithful, number-preserving translation of the technical results.
